title: Add pre-commit hook for vendor file protection
body:
Add a pre-commit hook that enforces the same vendor file protection as the check-vendor GHA workflow, vendored to consumer repos via install.sh.

## Design

- Add `--staged` flag to `vendored/check` — checks `git diff --cached --name-only` (staged files) instead of branch diff
- Create `vendored/hooks/pre-commit` — thin shell script calling `python3 .vendored/check --staged`
- Add `--install-hook` flag to `vendored/check` — symlinks `.vendored/hooks/pre-commit` into `.git/hooks/pre-commit`, force-overwriting any existing hook (including pearls)
- Update `install.sh` to download the hook to `.vendored/hooks/pre-commit`

## Acceptance criteria

### `--staged` mode
- [ ] `vendored/check --staged` checks staged files via `git diff --cached --name-only`
- [ ] Reuses all existing protection logic (patterns, allowed lists, install-branch skip)
- [ ] Exits 0 if no staged violations, 1 if violations found
- [ ] Works both in CI and locally (no dependency on GitHub env vars)

### `vendored/hooks/pre-commit`
- [ ] Shell script at `vendored/hooks/pre-commit` that runs `python3 .vendored/check --staged`
- [ ] Exits with the check script exit code
- [ ] Executable permissions set

### `--install-hook` mode
- [ ] `vendored/check --install-hook` symlinks `.git/hooks/pre-commit` to `.vendored/hooks/pre-commit` (relative: `../../.vendored/hooks/pre-commit`)
- [ ] Force-overwrites any existing pre-commit hook (file or symlink) — squashes pearls etc.
- [ ] Verifies `.vendored/hooks/pre-commit` exists before symlinking, errors if missing
- [ ] Prints confirmation message on success

### install.sh
- [ ] Downloads `vendored/hooks/pre-commit` to `.vendored/hooks/pre-commit`
- [ ] Sets executable permissions (`chmod +x`)
- [ ] Creates `.vendored/hooks/` directory

### Tests
- [ ] Unit tests for `--staged` mode in `tests/test_check.py` (staged files checked, protection logic applied, violations reported)
- [ ] Unit test for `--install-hook` (symlink created, overwrites existing, errors on missing hook file)

### Docs
- [ ] Update `install.sh` header comment to document the new hook file
- [ ] Add `--staged` and `--install-hook` to the check script docstring

title: Vendor contract v2: manifest-driven install/check/remove
body: Redesign the vendor contract around a manifest-based approach. Flatten repo structure, merge add+update into install, add remove command, and shift version/metadata ownership to the framework.

title: Directory Restructure: Consolidate Vendor Files Under .vendored/
body:
Consolidate per-vendor dotdirs (`.pearls/`, `.dogfood/`, `.semver/`) and the shared `config.json` into a structured layout under `.vendored/`:

- **`configs/<vendor>.json`** — per-vendor config (replaces monolithic `config.json`)
- **`pkg/<vendor>/`** — vendor-installed files (replaces per-vendor dotdirs at repo root)

This eliminates dotdir sprawl and merge conflicts from concurrent vendor update PRs.

**Depends on:** gv-cf3f (v2 contract — manifests, env vars) — all tasks closed.

title: Unify vendor registry and project config under _vendor key
body:
After gv-a34c lands, per-vendor configs live at `.vendored/configs/<vendor>.json` with registry fields at the top level. This epic introduces a `_vendor` namespace key to separate framework-managed registry fields from vendor-defined project config, then merges existing per-vendor project configs into the same files.

**Target schema:**
```json
{
  "_vendor": {
    "repo": "mangimangi/pearls",
    "install_branch": "chore/install-pearls",
    "automerge": true,
    "protected": [".vendored/pkg/pearls/**"],
    "allowed": [".vendored/configs/pearls.json"]
  },
  "prefix": "gv",
  "docs": ["AGENTS.md"],
  "models": { "implementer": "claude-opus-4-6" }
}
```

- `_vendor` — framework-owned, written by install/remove, read by check. Underscore signals "managed."
- Top-level keys — project-owned, opaque to framework, read by the vendor tool itself.

**Depends on:** gv-a34c (directory restructure — per-vendor configs and pkg/ layout).

title: Vendor Contract Validation GHA
body:
Expose vendor contract checks as a standalone validation tool and GitHub Action workflow. Allows answering "does repo XYZ validly implement the git-vendored vendor contract?" without actually installing the vendor.

See docs/planning/epics/gv-9d4e-validation-gha.md for full design.

title: Vendor dependency management
body:
Extend the vendor contract to support vendor-to-vendor dependencies. Vendors declare which other vendors they require via an optional deps.json at repo root, and the framework checks (and optionally installs) those dependencies during the install flow.

## Motivation

Today, if vendor A requires vendor B to function, there's no way to express or enforce this. The failure mode is silent: vendor A installs fine, then breaks at runtime because vendor B isn't present. Consumers have to debug missing deps themselves; vendor authors resort to README notes.

## Key Design Decisions

- **deps.json at repo root** (not emitted by install.sh) — deps must be checked before running install.sh
- **Match deps by repo field**, not vendor name — handles consumers who install with custom --name
- **Dependency modes**: error (default, safe), warn, install (auto-install), skip
- **Version constraints deferred** — v1 is presence-only
- **Topological sort for install all** — prevents ordering failures
- **Cache resolved deps** in .vendored/manifests/<vendor>.deps for reverse-dep lookups

## Planning Doc

docs/planning/dependency-management.md

title: Multi-vendor PR commit fails: pre-commit hook blocks chore/install-vendors branch
body:
## Problem

When multiple vendors update simultaneously (e.g., git-semver + git-vendored), `create_pull_request()` in `.vendored/install` fails with `CalledProcessError` on `git commit`.

**Root cause:** The multi-vendor branch name `chore/install-vendors` (line 636) does not match any individual vendor's `install_branch` prefix (e.g., `chore/install-git-vendored`, `chore/install-git-semver`). The pre-commit hook runs `.vendored/check --staged`, which checks branch matching at `check_vendor()` line 181 — since no prefix matches, it treats all staged vendor files as protection violations and exits 1.

Single-vendor updates work because their branch name (e.g., `chore/install-git-vendored-v0.0.11`) starts with the vendor's `install_branch`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `create_pull_request()` succeeds when multiple vendors update simultaneously
- [ ] The fix uses `git commit --no-verify` in `create_pull_request()` since the install script IS the authorized mechanism for modifying vendor files (the pre-commit hook guards against manual edits, not automated installs)
- [ ] Add a code comment explaining why `--no-verify` is used
- [ ] Add a test that verifies `create_pull_request` passes `--no-verify` to git commit
- [ ] Existing single-vendor PR creation still works

## Reproduction

Run `python3 .vendored/install all --pr` when 2+ vendors have updates available, with the pre-commit hook installed. The `git commit` step in `create_pull_request()` will fail with exit code 1.

## Files to Change

- `.vendored/install` line 681: add `--no-verify` flag to git commit
- `tests/` — add test coverage for multi-vendor commit with pre-commit hook

title: Use git push --force-with-lease for ephemeral install branches
body:
## Problem
`create_pull_request()` does a plain `git push -u origin <branch>`, which
fails as a non-fast-forward when the ephemeral install branch (e.g.
`chore/install-vendors`) already exists on the remote from a prior run.

## Fix
Change to `git push --force-with-lease -u origin <branch>`.

This is safe because these branches are ephemeral automation branches, not
shared work. `--force-with-lease` still guards against the edge case where
someone else pushed to the branch concurrently.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `templates/install` push command uses `--force-with-lease`
- [ ] `.vendored/install` push command uses `--force-with-lease`
- [ ] Brief inline comment explaining why force-with-lease is appropriate
- [ ] Test asserts `--force-with-lease` is present in the push command
- [ ] Existing tests still pass

title: Exclude workflow files from automated install PR commits
body:
## Problem
`create_pull_request()` runs `git add -A` which stages `.github/workflows/`
files written by install.sh. The `GITHUB_TOKEN` used in CI lacks `workflows`
permission, so `git push` is rejected with:

> refusing to allow a GitHub App to create or update workflow without
> `workflows` permission

## Root cause
install.sh always installs workflow templates (lines 112-113) and includes
them in the manifest. `git add -A` blindly stages everything. These workflow
files should only be installed during initial bootstrap, not re-committed in
automated update PRs.

## Fix
After `git add -A`, run `git reset HEAD -- .github/workflows/` to unstage
workflow files before committing. This keeps install.sh's behavior unchanged
(it still writes them to disk for initial installs) but excludes them from
the automated PR commit.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `create_pull_request()` unstages `.github/workflows/` after `git add -A`
- [ ] Both `templates/install` and `.vendored/install` are updated
- [ ] Test verifies `git reset HEAD -- .github/workflows/` is called
- [ ] Existing tests still pass

title: Self-bootstrap coordinator on git-vendored install
body:
## Problem
When any consumer repo updates git-vendored, the OLD `.vendored/install`
coordinator runs in memory while the NEW `install.sh` writes the fixed
coordinator to disk. Post-install steps (PR creation, output) execute
using the OLD in-memory code. If the OLD coordinator has a bug in those
post-install paths, the install fails, the fix never gets committed, and
the repo is stuck indefinitely.

Example: the force-push fix in `create_pull_request()` — install.sh wrote
the fixed coordinator to disk, but the old `create_pull_request()` was
still executing in memory.

## Root cause
`download_and_run_install()` runs the vendor's install.sh, which overwrites
`.vendored/install` on disk. But the Python process continues executing the
old version from memory. There is no mechanism to hand off to the new
coordinator for post-install steps.

## Solution
After `download_and_run_install()` returns, detect that the on-disk
`.vendored/install` has changed (hash comparison against `__file__`).
If changed, re-exec the process:

```
python3 .vendored/install git-vendored --pr --post-install-only
```

The `--post-install-only` flag tells the NEW coordinator to skip
`download_and_run_install()` and only run post-install steps (build
result from on-disk manifest/version state, create PR, write output).

### Flow
1. `python3 .vendored/install git-vendored --pr` (OLD coordinator)
2. OLD coordinator calls `download_and_run_install()` → new install.sh
   writes new coordinator to disk
3. OLD coordinator detects `.vendored/install` on disk differs from
   running script (hash comparison)
4. OLD coordinator re-execs:
   `python3 .vendored/install git-vendored --pr --post-install-only`
5. NEW coordinator sees `--post-install-only`, reads version/manifest
   from disk, creates PR

### Scope
This is specifically for git-vendored updating its own coordinator.
No other vendor's install.sh writes to `.vendored/install`, so no
generalization is needed. Detection should key off whether the on-disk
coordinator actually changed, not vendor name or dogfood flag.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Add `--post-install-only` flag to CLI argument parser
- [ ] After `download_and_run_install()`, hash-compare `__file__` against
      on-disk `.vendored/install`; if changed, re-exec with `--post-install-only`
      and forward all relevant flags (--pr, --version, --force)
- [ ] `--post-install-only` code path reads installed version and manifest
      from disk, builds result dict, runs PR creation and output
- [ ] Guard against infinite re-exec (e.g. if `--post-install-only` is set,
      never re-exec again)
- [ ] Add tests for the bootstrap detection and re-exec path
- [ ] Update docs if install CLI reference exists
